section: Effects of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high specific hardness

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the abstract, introduction, materials and methods, and initial hardness data. It establishes three Fe-35Mn-xAl-5Cu (x=15, 17, 19 at.%) low-density steels designated 15Al, 17Al, 19Al with composition in Table 1. Processing: vacuum induction melting, homogenization 1200°C/24h, hot rolling to 4 mm, solution treatment 1100°C/10min + oil quench, aging 525–650°C for 1–10h + water quench. Densities: 6.85, 6.76, 6.66 g/cm³. Tables 2–4 and Fig. 2 provide hardness evolution; peak hardness values are 965.6 HV (15Al, 600°C/6h), 924.9 HV (17Al, 575°C/6h), 917.8 HV (19Al, 600°C/10h). Abstract claims that increasing Al slows β-Mn kinetics, slightly raises β-Mn intrinsic hardness, reduces FCC Cu-rich phase at β-Mn interfaces and fracture toughness, decreasing final hardening. 15Al reaches specific hardness 141 HV·cm³/g. Fracture toughness to be evaluated by indentation (Fig. 1, Eq. 1). Prior β-Mn steels had hardness <800 HV. The remaining manuscript will detail aging curves, microstructure (β-Mn, Cu-rich phase) evolution via SEM/EBSD/TEM, and intrinsic hardness measurements.
processing_route: solution treatment at 1100°C for 10 min + oil quench
aging at 525–650°C for 1–10 h + water quench
microstructure: FCC Cu-rich phase at β-Mn interfaces
mechanisms: Al slows β-Mn precipitation kinetics
Al slightly increases β-Mn intrinsic hardness
Al reduces FCC Cu-rich phase and fracture toughness, lowering hardening effect
unresolved_terms: FCC Cu-rich phase distribution and role
fracture toughness values for peak-aged conditions

context_update.summary: This chunk describes microstructure characterization methods (SEM, EBSD, TEM) and presents Results on aging hardening behaviors and phase evolution for Fe-35Mn-xAl-5Cu (x=15,17,19 at.%) low-density steels. Hardness data (Fig. 2, Tables 2-4) show 15Al achieves 253.4–965.6 HV, 17Al 259.8–924.9 HV, and 19Al 263.8–917.8 HV, with peaks at 600°C/6h (965.6 HV), 575°C/6h (924.9 HV), and 600°C/10h (917.8 HV), respectively. Aging at ≥575°C strongly accelerates hardening; higher Al content retards the hardening response and shifts the optimal aging window upward. EBSD (Figs. 3-6) reveals solution-treated alloys are single α (BCC), and extensive β-Mn precipitation drives hardening. The 15Al alloy forms 85.8% β-Mn after 525°C/3h; α fully transforms at higher temperatures, with new fine BCC and FCC precipitates appearing within β-Mn. The 17Al and 19Al alloys show slower precipitation (20.5% and 12.1% β-Mn at 525°C/10h) and also develop BCC/FCC phases after full transformation. SE images (Figs. 7-9) illustrate α-Mn as fine particles and short rod-shaped β-Mn, with α-Mn contributing limited hardening. XRD (Fig. 10) confirms α-Mn/β-Mn co-precipitation before complete transformation. Future sections should integrate these phase evolution details with intrinsic hardness and fracture toughness discussions.
mechanisms: β-Mn causes strong hardening
α-Mn gives limited hardening
Al retards β-Mn precipitation kinetics
solid-solution strengthening increases solution-treated hardness with Al
unresolved_terms: detailed role of new BCC and FCC precipitates
TEM observations of β-Mn and Cu-rich phase

section: 3.2. Evolutions of constituent phases after different aging treatments

Hardness data (Fig. 2, Tables 2-4) show 15Al achieves 253.4–965.6 HV, 17Al 259.8–924.9 HV, and 19Al 263.8–917.8 HV, with peaks at 600°C/6h (965.6 HV), 575°C/6h (924.9 HV), and 600°C/10h (917.8 HV), respectively. Aging at ≥575°C strongly accelerates hardening; higher Al content retards the hardening response and shifts the optimal aging window upward. EBSD (Figs. 3-6) reveals solution-treated alloys are single α (BCC), and extensive β-Mn precipitation drives hardening. The 15Al alloy forms 85.8% β-Mn after 525°C/3h; α fully transforms at higher temperatures, with new fine BCC and FCC precipitates appearing within β-Mn. The 17Al and 19Al alloys show slower precipitation (20.5% and 12.1% β-Mn at 525°C/10h) and also develop BCC/FCC phases after full transformation. SE images (Figs. 7-9) illustrate α-Mn as fine particles and short rod-shaped β-Mn, with α-Mn contributing limited hardening. XRD (Fig. 10) confirms α-Mn/β-Mn co-precipitation before complete transformation. context_update.summary: This chunk reports SEM observations (Figs. 8,9) for 17Al and 19Al alloys, showing that increasing Al retards β-Mn precipitation kinetics: at 525°C, β-Mn is minimal after 10 h, mainly on grain boundaries; at 550°C, full transformation requires 6 h for 17Al and 10 h for 19Al; at ≥600°C, 1 h completes the transformation. Fine precipitates appear within β-Mn after full transformation. XRD (Fig.10) confirms co-precipitation of α-Mn and β-Mn before complete transformation. TEM (Figs.11,12) on peak-hardness samples (15Al 600°C/6h, 17Al 575°C/6h) reveals near-equiaxed β-Mn with fine FCC Cu-rich particles at interfaces, Cu-rich phase FCC twins, and β-Mn poor in Cu. Nanoindentation (Fig.13) gives β-Mn intrinsic hardnesses of 12.1±0.3 GPa (15Al), 12.2±0.1 GPa (17Al), 12.5±0.4 GPa (19Al); the slight increase is due to solid-solution strengthening by higher Al in β-Mn (14.3, 16.3, 17.5 at.%). However, increasing Al promotes lower-hardness FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases, reducing overall Vickers hardness (965.6→917.8 HV). Fracture toughness KIC drops with Al: 8.6, 6.6, 3.5 MPa·m^1/2. These microstructural and property data are essential for the subsequent discussion of precipitation kinetics and strengthening-toughness trade-offs.
processing_route: aging at 525–650°C for 1–10 h water quench
microstructure: FCC Cu-rich phase (Cu-rich, twin-containing, at β-Mn interfaces)
fine precipitates inside β-Mn after full transformation
properties: peak Vickers hardness: 965.6 HV (15Al), 924.9 HV (17Al), 917.8 HV (19Al)
mechanisms: Al retards β-Mn precipitation kinetics
Al slightly raises β-Mn intrinsic hardness via solid-solution strengthening
FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases have lower intrinsic hardness than β-Mn, reducing overall hardness
higher Al promotes FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases
higher Al reduces fracture toughness

context_update.summary: This chunk (Discussion 4.1) establishes that complete α→β-Mn transformation is essential for ultra-high hardness. It quantifies how increasing Al content retards β-Mn precipitation kinetics: 15Al completes transformation at 525°C/3h, while 17Al and 19Al require 550°C/6h and 10h respectively. Two mechanisms are proposed: Al enrichment around β-Mn and Al-stabilized D03 phase that must first dissolve. The overall microstructure evolution is defined. β-Mn volume fraction directly boosts hardness, with intrinsic hardness over 10 GPa. Although Al slightly raises β-Mn intrinsic hardness, it mainly increases softer BCC and FCC Cu-rich phases at β-Mn interfaces, reducing β-Mn volume fraction and thus overall hardness. Data for 650°C aged samples: 15Al/3h → 3.9% precipitates, 852.3 HV; 17Al/10h → 4.7%, 833.2 HV; 19Al/6h → 6.5%, 810.9 HV. Fracture toughness KIC drops with Al due to harder β-Mn and fewer ductile FCC Cu-rich phases. Figures 13 (nanoindentation), 15 (EBSD maps at 525°C), and 16 (specific hardness benchmark) are cited. Future sections should link these kinetics, phase fraction trends and KIC data to final strengthening models.

section: 4.1. Effect of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels
previous_context_summary:
Summary: Fracture toughness KIC drops with Al: 8.6, 6.6, 3.5 MPa·m^1/2. These microstructural and property data are essential for the subsequent discussion of precipitation kinetics and strengthening-toughness trade-offs. This chunk (Discussion 4.1) establishes that complete α→β-Mn transformation is essential for ultra-high hardness. It quantifies how increasing Al content retards β-Mn precipitation kinetics: 15Al completes transformation at 525°C/3h, while 17Al and 19Al require 550°C/6h and 10h respectively. Two mechanisms are proposed: Al enrichment around β-Mn and Al-stabilized D03 phase that must first dissolve. The overall microstructure evolution is defined. β-Mn volume fraction directly boosts hardness, with intrinsic hardness over 10 GPa. Although Al slightly raises β-Mn intrinsic hardness, it mainly increases softer BCC and FCC Cu-rich phases at β-Mn interfaces, reducing β-Mn volume fraction and thus overall hardness. Data for 650°C aged samples: 15Al/3h → 3.9% precipitates, 852.3 HV; 17Al/10h → 4.7%, 833.2 HV; 19Al/6h → 6.5%, 810.9 HV. Fracture toughness KIC drops with Al due to harder β-Mn and fewer ductile FCC Cu-rich phases. Figures 13 (nanoindentation), 15 (EBSD maps at 525°C), and 16 (specific hardness benchmark) are cited. Future sections should link these kinetics, phase fraction trends and KIC data to final strengthening models.

Processing route: vacuum induction melting; homogenization at 1200°C for 24 h; hot rolling to 4 mm; solution treatment at 1100°C for 10 min + oil quench; aging at 525–650°C for 1–10 h + water quench; solution treatment 1100°C 10 min oil quench; aging at 525–650°C for 1–10 h water quench; peak-aging: 15Al 600°C/6h, 17Al 575°C/6h, 19Al 600°C/10h
Key variables: Al content: 15, 17, 19 at.%; aging temperature; aging time; Al content; β-Mn volume fraction; Al content (15, 17, 19 at.%); aging temperature (525, 550, 575, 600°C); aging time (1, 3, 6, 10 h)
Microstructure: β-Mn precipitation; FCC Cu-rich phase at β-Mn interfaces; α BCC; β-Mn; α-Mn; FCC phase; BCC precipitates; β-Mn fraction up to 85.8% in 15Al
Properties: density: 6.85 (15Al), 6.76 (17Al), 6.66 (19Al) g/cm³; Vickers hardness range 253.4–965.6 HV; peak hardness 965.6 HV (15Al), 924.9 HV (17Al), 917.8 HV (19Al); specific hardness 141 HV·cm³/g (15Al); fracture toughness via indentation; peak hardness 965.6 HV (15Al 600°C/6h); hardness 872.0 HV (15Al 525°C/3h); peak Vickers hardness: 965.6 HV (15Al), 924.9 HV (17Al), 917.8 HV (19Al)
Mechanisms: Al slows β-Mn precipitation kinetics; Al slightly increases β-Mn intrinsic hardness; Al reduces FCC Cu-rich phase and fracture toughness, lowering hardening effect; β-Mn causes strong hardening; α-Mn gives limited hardening; Al retards β-Mn precipitation kinetics; solid-solution strengthening increases solution-treated hardness with Al; Al slightly raises β-Mn intrinsic hardness via solid-solution strengthening

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Section 4.2 Insights and Section 5 Conclusions. It summarizes the broad adjustable hardness range (253.4–965.6 HV) achieved via simple aging. A specific hardness comparison is presented in Fig. 16, showing that Fe-Mn-Al-Cu steels exceed 140 HV·cm³/g, outperforming existing steels and M/HEAs (typically <800 HV, <120 HV·cm³/g). The 15Al alloy maintains fracture toughness of 8.6 MPa·m^1/2 at 965.5 HV. Ultra-high hardness >950 HV arises from continuous β-Mn precipitation during short aging; alloys can be shaped in the soft solution-treated state then aged. Al content affects precipitation kinetics: higher Al slows β-Mn precipitation, delays α→β transformation, slightly increases intrinsic β-Mn hardness but reduces the FCC Cu-rich phase at β-Mn interfaces and lowers fracture toughness, thereby decreasing overall hardening. Conclusions restate that the 15Al alloy attains the maximum specific hardness of 141 HV·cm³/g. Further optimization of hardness–toughness balance is needed. Key figure: Fig. 16.
key_variables: fracture toughness (8.6 MPa·m^1/2 at 965.5 HV for 15Al)
microstructure: FCC Cu-rich phase at β-Mn interfaces
mechanisms: Al slows β-Mn precipitation kinetics, extending α→β transformation
Al slightly increases β-Mn intrinsic hardness but reduces FCC Cu-rich phase and fracture toughness, lowering hardening effect
continuous β-Mn precipitation enables wide-range tunable hardness
